name: The 12 Cranial Nerves
description: The 12 cranial nerves drilled both ways: number to name and name to number, plus what each one does and whether it is sensory, motor, or both. Includes the two classic medical-school mnemonics.

fields.front: CN I
fields.extra: Cranial nerve 1. Sensory. Carries the sense of smell.

fields.front: CN II
fields.extra: Cranial nerve 2. Sensory. Carries vision from the retina.

fields.front: CN III
fields.extra: Cranial nerve 3. Motor. Moves the eye and eyelid, constricts the pupil, and focuses the lens.

fields.front: CN IV
fields.extra: Cranial nerve 4. Motor. Rotates the eye downward and inward via the superior oblique muscle.

fields.front: CN V
fields.extra: Cranial nerve 5. Both. Provides sensation to the face and powers chewing.

fields.front: CN VI
fields.extra: Cranial nerve 6. Motor. Turns the eye outward (abduction) via the lateral rectus muscle.

fields.front: CN VII
fields.extra: Cranial nerve 7. Both. Controls facial expression, taste on the front of the tongue, and tears and saliva.

fields.front: CN VIII
fields.extra: Cranial nerve 8. Sensory. Carries hearing and balance.

fields.front: CN IX
fields.extra: Cranial nerve 9. Both. Handles taste and sensation at the back of the tongue, swallowing, and salivation.

fields.front: CN X
fields.extra: Cranial nerve 10. Both. Provides parasympathetic control of the heart, lungs, and gut, plus voice and swallowing.

fields.front: CN XI
fields.extra: Cranial nerve 11. Motor. Moves the head and shoulders via the sternocleidomastoid and trapezius muscles.

fields.front: CN XII
fields.extra: Cranial nerve 12. Motor. Moves the tongue.

fields.front: What is the main job of the olfactory nerve (CN I)?
fields.extra: Sensory nerve. The only cranial nerve that does not relay through the thalamus before reaching the cortex; its fibers pass through the cribriform plate of the ethmoid bone.

fields.front: What is the main job of the optic nerve (CN II)?
fields.extra: Sensory nerve. Really an outgrowth of the brain wrapped in meninges, not a true peripheral nerve; the two optic nerves cross at the optic chiasm.

fields.front: What is the main job of the oculomotor nerve (CN III)?
fields.back: Moves the eye and eyelid, constricts the pupil, and focuses the lens
fields.extra: Motor nerve. Controls four of the six muscles that move the eye plus the muscle that raises the lid. Damage gives a 'down and out' eye, a drooping lid, and a blown pupil.

fields.front: What is the main job of the trochlear nerve (CN IV)?
fields.back: Rotates the eye downward and inward via the superior oblique muscle
fields.extra: Motor nerve. The thinnest cranial nerve and the only one to emerge from the back of the brainstem; palsy causes vertical double vision that is worst looking down (trouble on stairs).

fields.front: What is the main job of the trigeminal nerve (CN V)?
fields.back: Provides sensation to the face and powers chewing
fields.extra: Mixed nerve (sensory and motor). The largest cranial nerve, with three divisions: ophthalmic (V1), maxillary (V2), and mandibular (V3). Only V3 carries the motor fibers to the muscles of mastication.

fields.front: What is the main job of the abducens nerve (CN VI)?
fields.back: Turns the eye outward (abduction) via the lateral rectus muscle
fields.extra: Motor nerve. Has the longest course inside the skull, so it is often the first nerve squeezed by rising intracranial pressure; palsy causes horizontal double vision.

fields.front: What is the main job of the facial nerve (CN VII)?
fields.back: Controls facial expression, taste on the front of the tongue, and tears and saliva
fields.extra: Mixed nerve (sensory and motor). Motor to the muscles of facial expression; it also carries taste from the front two-thirds of the tongue and parasympathetics to the tear and salivary glands. Its paralysis is Bell's palsy.

fields.front: What is the main job of the vestibulocochlear nerve (CN VIII)?
fields.extra: Sensory nerve. Also called the auditory or acoustic nerve; the cochlear part carries hearing and the vestibular part carries balance. A tumor here (acoustic neuroma) causes hearing loss and vertigo.

fields.front: What is the main job of the glossopharyngeal nerve (CN IX)?
fields.back: Handles taste and sensation at the back of the tongue, swallowing, and salivation
fields.extra: Mixed nerve (sensory and motor). Carries taste and sensation from the back third of the tongue, the sensing limb of the gag reflex, and parasympathetics to the parotid salivary gland.

fields.front: What is the main job of the vagus nerve (CN X)?
fields.back: Provides parasympathetic control of the heart, lungs, and gut, plus voice and swallowing
fields.extra: Mixed nerve (sensory and motor). The longest cranial nerve; 'vagus' means wandering. It reaches nearly every organ in the chest and abdomen and drives 'rest and digest' responses.

fields.front: What is the main job of the accessory nerve (CN XI)?
fields.back: Moves the head and shoulders via the sternocleidomastoid and trapezius muscles
fields.extra: Motor nerve. Also called the spinal accessory nerve because it arises largely from the upper spinal cord. Damage causes a drooping shoulder and weakness turning the head.

fields.front: What is the main job of the hypoglossal nerve (CN XII)?
fields.extra: Motor nerve. Supplies almost all the tongue muscles; when it is damaged, the tongue deviates toward the injured side when stuck out.

fields.front: What is the classic mnemonic for the names of the 12 cranial nerves, in order?
fields.back: On Old Olympus' Towering Tops, A Finn And German Viewed Some Hops.
fields.extra: Olfactory, Optic, Oculomotor, Trochlear, Trigeminal, Abducens, Facial, Auditory (vestibulocochlear), Glossopharyngeal, Vagus, Accessory (spinal), Hypoglossal.

fields.front: What is the mnemonic for whether each cranial nerve is Sensory, Motor, or Both, in order (I to XII)?
fields.back: Some Say Marry Money, But My Brother Says Big Brains Matter Most.
fields.extra: S S M M B M B S B B M M: Sensory (I, II), Motor (III, IV), Both (V), Motor (VI), Both (VII), Sensory (VIII), Both (IX, X), Motor (XI, XII).
